As ballast in Phase III is no doubt unacceptable and needs immediate remedial action, ballast in Phase I and II is particularly worth studying from the aspect of how different fouling scenarios would affect ballast strength and therefore impact track stability. Contact forces [8] between individual aggregate particles are illustrated in Fig. 2 . A contact force can be decomposed into the normal and shear force components. The magnitudes of contact forces depend on the external loading. DEM theory [8] assumes that there exists a shear contact strength (denoted as ‘‘SCS’’ in Fig. 2) between two aggregate particles in contact, which is a func- tion of normal contact force and the aggregate surface texture (i.e. surface friction angle). If the contact shear force (introduced by external loading) between two particles exceeds this contact shear strength, aggregates will start sliding against each other, and otherwise, they will remain contact. This surface friction angle is denoted by ‘‘ h’’. If a ballast sample is clean (shown in the left part of Fig. 2), the surface friction angle is determined by the surface roughness of the aggregate itself. However, in Phase II fouling condition, due to the existence of fouling agent, the surface friction angle ‘‘ h’’ will be af- fected or even dominated by the fouling agent (shown in the right) and becomes the ‘‘fouled surface friction angle (‘‘ h’’)’’. Both ‘‘ h’’ and ‘‘ h 0’’ and the corresponding ballast behaviors need to be deter- mined and investigated by combining laboratory tests and numer- ical modeling techniques such as ‘‘Image-aided DEM’’. 3. Image-aided DEM approach Discrete Element Method is a numerical modeling approach to solve problems that exhibit gross discontinuous behavior. These methods are able to analyze multiple interacting bodies undergo- ing large dynamic movements. By modeling the individual parti- cles and computing their motion, the overall behavior of the granular assembly, which may include unrecoverable deforma- tions, dilation, post-peak behavior, and anisotropy, is modeled implicitly. Interaction of granular materials or rock masses can this way be modeled accurately and realistically since any discontinu- ous detail can be included in the analysis. The DEM is believed to be one of the most promising tools to simulate ballast behavior. An image-aided DEM approach, which utilizes a DEM program BLOKS3D developed at the University of Illinois [9], has been re- cently introduced to investigate effects of multi-scale aggregate morphological properties on performances of railroad ballast lay- ers [10,11]. Imaging technology provides detailed measurements of aggregate shape, texture and angularity properties and has been successfully used in the last two decades for quantifying aggregate morphology. Among the various particle morphological indices, the ﬂat and elongated (F&E) ratio, the angularity index (AI), and the surface texture (ST) index, all developed using University of Illi- nois Aggregate Image Analyzer (UIAIA), are key indices [12,13]. The UIAIA system features taking images of an individual aggregate particle from three orthogonal views to quantify imaging based F&E ratio, AI, and ST morphological indices. The image-aided DEM approach then recreates the three-dimensional

Discrete Element Modeling for fouled railroad ballast Hai Huang a,⇑, Erol Tutumluer b a Penn State University, Altoona, PA 16601, United States b University of Illinois, Urbana, IL 61801, United States article info Article history: Received 29 December 2010 Received in revised form 28 February 2011 Accepted 1 March 2011 Available online 31 March 2011 Keywords: Discrete Element Modeling Railroad ballast Fouling Coal dust Half-track abstract Fouling refers to the condition of railroad ballast when voids in this unbound aggregate layer are ﬁlled with relatively ﬁner materials or fouling agents commonly from the ballast aggregate breakdown, outside contamination such as coal dust from coal trains, or from subgrade soil intrusion. This paper aims to investigate the effect of fouling on ballast strength and stability. Fouling mechanism was ﬁrst explained followed by investigation of mechanical properties of fouling agent. Coal dust was chosen as the fouling agent due to its poor mechanical properties and it had been reported to mostly decrease aggregate assembly strength comparing to other fouling agents. An image-aided Discrete Element Modeling (DEM) approach was introduced to simulate the coal dust fouled track ﬁeld settlement performance. To that end, DEM model parameters for clean and coal dust fouled ballast were validated by matching the DEM direct shear box simulation results to the laboratory shear box testing results for both samples. By assigning laboratory calibrated model parameters to the ‘‘half-track’’ ballast sample generated in DEM, effects of different fouling percentage as well as different fouling locations on ballast settlement perfor- mance are studied. Results from the ‘‘half-track’’ DEM simulation revealed that fouling could lead to unfa- vorable track distresses such as ‘‘hanging tie’’. Further, shoulder fouling scenario was proven to be critical in track maintenance. Crown Copyright /C2112011 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Fouling refers to the condition of railroad ballast when voids in this unbound aggregate layer are ﬁlled with relatively ﬁner mate- rials. Fouling agents are commonly from the ballast aggregate breakdown, outside contamination such as coal dust from coal trains, or from subgrade soil intrusion. Fouling materials in ballast have been traditionally considered not favorable for railroad bal- last performance [1]. Early research studies reported that around 70% of the fouling materials were from ballast breakdown [2–4]. Railroad company internal studies also noted that almost all foul- ing ﬁnes in the railroad track were commonly from aggregate breakdown [5]. According to Selig and Waters [6], ballast break- down on the average accounts for up to 76% of the ballast fouling followed by 13% inﬁltration from subballast, 7% inﬁltration from ballast surface, 3% subgrade intrusion, and 1% due to tie wear. Recent research by Huang et al. [7] showed that coal dust was by far the worst fouling agent for its impact on track substructure and roadbed. Approximately 15% coal dust fouling by weight of ballast was statistically signiﬁcant to cause considerable strength reductions. In the case of ballast fully fouled with wet coal dust at 35% optimum moisture content, the friction angles obtained were as low as the friction angle of coal dust itself. Therefore, in this paper, coal dust fouled track behavior in the ﬁeld condition is studied. Ballast fouled by coal dust at different locat

vice is a square box with side dimensions of 12 in. (305 mm) and a specimen height of 203 mm (8 in.). It has a total 102 mm (4-in.) travel of the bottom 152 mm (6-in.) high component which is large enough for ballast testing purposes to record peak shear stresses. The vertical (normal direction) and horizontal load cells are capa- ble of applying and recording up to 50-kN load magnitudes. The device controls and the data collection are managed through an automated data acquisition system controlled by the operator through a built-in display and the test data are saved onto a per- sonal computer. Three normal stresses (172, 241, and 310 kPa) and a constant shearing rate of 0.2 mm/s (0.48 in./min) were used in the laboratory tests. Ballast sample preparation process is intro- duced as following: 1. Place aggregates in the lower box by lifts (usually two 76 mm lifts). 2. For each lift, use vibratory compactor on top of a ﬂat Plexiglas compaction platform and compact until no noticeable move- ment of particles is observed. 3. Record the weight of aggregate used. The ballast material tested was a typical AREMA No. 24 graded granite aggregate with a speciﬁc gravity of 2.62 obtained from Gillette, WY and commonly used in the Powder River Basin (PRB) joint line. Aggregates were processed through UIAIA for shape characterizing and regenerating representative particle shapes in BLOKS3D. Fig. 5 shows both the laboratory direct shear box test set up and the DEM model. 4.1. Calibration of clean ballast model Calibration of the DEM model involves the process of matching the simulation results to the laboratory results by adjusting the model parameters. Since the element shape and size distribution has already been determined, the adjustable model parameters are: normal contact stiffness, shear contact stiffness, and surface friction angle. Fig. 6 shows the shear reaction stresses plotted against the shear displacements under different normal stresses for both laboratory tests and DEM simulations on clean ballast samples. Fig. 3. Comparisons of an actual aggregate particle with the discrete element representation generated for BLOKS3D DEM program. Fig. 4. Major BLOKS3D model parameters. Fig. 5. Direct shear box test laboratory setup and DEM model. 3308 H. Huang, E. Tutumluer / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 3306–3312

DEM simulation results are plotted in dash lines in Fig. 6 . For DEM simulation, after trial and error, the normal contact stiffness is adjusted to 20 MN/m; the shear contact stiffness value is ad- justed to 10 MN/m; and the surface friction angle is ﬁnally ad- justed to 31 /C176. From Fig. 6 , it may be concluded that the DEM simulation results based on this set of parameters can match the laboratory test results well. Therefore, it is decided to use this set of parameters to represent clean granite ballast sample in the rest part of this paper. Fig. 7 shows contact forces generated among aggregate particles in direct shear DEM simulation. It can be observed that the contact forces form a band connecting the upper left to the lower right, which reveals the fact that the shear strength of the ballast sample is mainly supplied by this force band. Furthermore, it may also im- ply that fouling agent existing in any locations along this shear force band will affect the shear strength. 4.2. Calibration of fully fouled ballast model Coal dust fouled ballast samples were prepared by adding coal dust into the clean ballast samples. Different fouling severities can be represented by adding different percentages of coal dust by the weight of clean aggregate. In another words, in the direct shear box tests, the coal dust ﬁlling depth determines the fouling severity. Fully coal dust fouled ballast samples (i.e. the coal dust ﬁlls all the way up to the top of the shear box) were prepared and tested for calibration purposes. A direct way to simulate fouled ballast in DEM is to add small sized elements (representing fouling materials such as coal dust) into large sized elements (representing clean ballast aggregates). It is however not economical in terms of computation time, since the smallest fouling material particle is around 0.075 mm and it may require hundreds and thousands of such particles in a large shear box test simulation. An alternative way to simulate ballast fouling is proposed herein. Based on previous laboratory testing results [7] it can be seen that coal dust is acting as a lubricant which, in another words, is similar to the case of decreasing element surface friction angle in the DEM model. Instead of adding smaller elements, a decreased surface friction angle is used for fouled contacts, which needs to be obtained from the similar calibration process as the clean bal- last sample. Fig. 8 shows the shear reaction force plotted against the shear displacement for both laboratory test and DEM simulation on fully fouled ballast sample. For DEM simulation, after trial and error, the surface friction angle is decreased to 27 /C176to match the laboratory results. The normal and shear contact stiffness values are kept the same based on the assumption that the coal dust will not greatly affect the contact stiffness. It is reasonable in terms of the deﬁnition we described previously for Phase II fouling. In Phase I and II, aggregates are not separated yet i.e. the contact stiffness can still be treated as unchanged. However, once aggregates start Fig. 6. Shear reaction forces from DEM direct shear simulations with surface friction angle of 31 /C176and results from laboratory clean samples. DEM Simulation of Fouled Track Upper Shear Box Lower Shear Box Contact Force Band Fig. 7. Contact force band among aggregate in direct shear box test. Fig. 8. Shear reaction forces from DEM direct shear simulations with surface friction angle of 27 /C176a

sliding against each other, they are sliding on a coal dust ﬁlm lubri- cated surface i.e. lower ‘‘surface friction angle’’. It is worth noticing that the ‘‘surface friction angle’’ is different than ‘‘angle of internal friction’’. The ‘‘surface friction angle’’ is a local contact surface property; whereas the ‘‘angle of internal friction’’ is the overall strength parameter. From Fig. 8 , it may be concluded that the DEM simulation results based on this set of parameters can match the laboratory test results well. Therefore, it is decided to use this set of parameters to represent coal dust fouled granite ballast sam- ple in the rest part of this paper. 4.3. Validation of fouling model The purpose of calibrating clean and fully coal dust fouled bal- last model is to predict the strength of any partially fouled ballast samples. For instance, in the case that only the lower half depth of ballast is fouled by coal dust, we can assign the fouled surface fric- tion angle (27 /C176) to the particles within this 3 in. depth and assign the clean surface friction angle (31 /C176) to the rest aggregate particles. To test the validity of this statement and DEM model parameters for clean and fouled ballast direct shear box tests on partially fouled ballast sample were conducted. Solid lines in Fig. 9 are the shear stress plotted against the shear displacement under three normal stresses for 15% coal dust fouled ballast sample in the laboratory. In this case, 15% fouling volumet- rically equals to the condition that the coal dust ﬁlls up the voids within the lower half of the lower shear box. During the DEM prediction, ballast aggregate particles within this region will be assigned with a surface friction angle of 27 /C176; whereas, parameters for particles in other part of the shear box are assigned with a sur- face friction angle of 31 /C176. The dash lines in Fig. 9 are shear stress plotted against shear displacement under three normal stresses predicted by DEM. It can be seen the DEM fouling model can pre- dict the laboratory results fairly accurately. 5. DEM simulation for fouled ballast ﬁeld performance Using both the clean granite ballast and fouled granite ballast models, any ﬁeld ballast conditions including clean, partially fouled, and fouled in different ballast layer arrangements can be modeled. In the following section, track settlement DEM simulations are conducted under repeated loading for two differ- ent fouling scenarios, shoulder fouling and track center fouling. A half-track model is generated with the layout shown in Fig. 10 . In Fig. 10 , a tie is placed on the top of a 0.5 m thick ballast sec- tion. Only half width of the track was modeled due to the symme- try of the structure. The dimension in the train moving direction was chosen to be 0.6 m, about one-tie spacing. All half-track ballast samples were compacted by the same effort. This was accom- plished by creating a DEM block element covering the top of the ballast and pushing downward with a force of 100 kN until no par- ticle movement was observed. The railroad tie was then generated and placed on top of the ballast. Fig. 11 gives the load proﬁle uti- lized to apply repeatedly on top of the tie, which was derived from a dynamic track model [14]. The half ballast section is further divided into four parts (0, 1, 2, and 3) which are named as: shoulder bottom, center bottom, shoulder top, and center top, respectively. In DEM simulations, dif- ferent parts can be assigned wit

assigned with a surface friction angle of 27 /C176, a 15% shoulder fouling scenario is simulated. The number ‘‘15%’’ comes from the previous calibration condition of 15% coal dust by weight ﬁlls up to the mid depth of the lower box in the direct shear box tests. Similarly, full shoulder fouling scenario can be achieved by assigning the fouling surface friction angle of 27 /C176to both ‘‘0’’ and ‘‘2’’ parts of the track. Fig. 12 is a snapshot of the half-track DEM model. After sample preparation, load was applied on the top of the tie. Fig. 13 shows the DEM predictions of ballast settlement after 120 cycles for different fouling scenarios. Settlements predicted after 120 cycles for all samples are also listed in Table 1 . For the ﬁrst hundred cycles of trafﬁc, Table 1 clearly gives that the clean ballast yields the smallest amount of settlement. This means fouling will increase the track settlement potential rela- tively fast. In other words, a fouled track portion will accumulate more settlement than clean track within relatively short amount of time which may lead to the scenario often referred as ‘‘hanging tie’’ illustrated in Fig. 14 . Without the lateral resistance from the interface of tie and ballast, the track is more prone to track buckle. Among all fouling scenarios, shoulder fouling is shown to have more detrimental effect on track settlement. In both partially fo- uled and fully fouled cases, shoulder fouling yields more settle- ment than center track fouling. This is explained in the following paragraph. In the half-track model, the ballast is conﬁned in three direc- tions: the train moving direction and the center plane. Therefore, the ballast settlement is mainly contributed by the vertical move- ment of the aggregate right underneath the tie and the lateral movement of aggregates in the shoulder part. The vertical move- ment of aggregate is typically due to the consolidation of the aggregates under repeated loading. The lateral movement is mostly caused by the particle ﬂow to the off track direction in the shoul- der. Since the center portion of the ballast is conﬁned in all direc- tions it will accumulate less settlement. Furthermore, the majority of the shear failure sliding surface is located in the shoulder part especially the shoulder bottom part; the shoulder ballast will expe- rience more displacement along the shearing direction which eventually produces more settlement. Fig. 12. Half-track DEM model with different parts fouled by coal dust. Fig. 13. DEM track settlement predictions for different fouling scenarios. Hanging tie scenario due to ballast fouling. H. Huang, E. Tutumluer / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 3306–3312 3311

6. Conclusions In this paper, fouled ballast behavior in the ﬁeld is investigated by the image-aided DEM approach. Fouling mechanism is ﬁrst de- scribed by characterizing different phases of fouling. It is seen that fouling agent is a critical factor which could greatly affect the aggregate to aggregate contacts. As the worst fouling agent, coal dust is chosen as the fouling agent to be studied. Based on the lab- oratory ﬁndings, a fouled ballast aggregate model was proposed and the model parameters were calibrated and validated by com- paring laboratory and DEM simulation results. Several ﬁeld fouling scenarios were then studied by assigning different DEM parame- ters to different portions of ballast. Results from a ‘‘half-track’’ DEM simulation revealed that fouling could lead to unfavorable track distresses such as ‘‘hanging tie’’. Further, shoulder fouling scenario was proven to be critical in track maintenance. References [1] Raymond GP. Design for railroad ballast and sub grade support. J Geotech Eng Div 1978 [January].
